title: Centralized Data Management System for Cloud Marketing Hub
description:
## Problem

Cloud Marketing Hub relied on manual processes to handle over 500,000 marketing emails every month. This caused slow task turnaround, siloed teams, and weak data governance across a 400+ employee organization — creating security risks and operational bottlenecks.

## Solution

I architected a centralized data management platform that automates email analysis and reporting, enforces granular role-based access control (RBAC), and hardens authentication with Telegram-based two-factor authentication (2FA). The system turns raw email data into actionable dashboards while ensuring only the right people access the right data.

## Architecture

- **Backend:** Django REST API with PostgreSQL for relational storage and Redis for caching and background task processing.
- **Frontend:** Next.js with TailwindCSS for a responsive, real-time admin dashboard.
- **Security:** Custom RBAC middleware paired with Telegram Bot API for 2FA code delivery.

## Challenges

The biggest challenge was building a pipeline that reliably ingests and processes half a million emails per month without data loss or delays. Designing RBAC rules that mirrored the company's complex organizational hierarchy required deep collaboration with stakeholders. Integrating Telegram 2FA without disrupting existing login flows demanded careful UX trade-offs.

## Results

- **65% reduction** in average task completion time
- **80% less manual data handling**, freeing analysts for higher-value work
- Secure governance framework adopted by **400+ employees** across all departments
- Eliminated unauthorized data access incidents through RBAC enforcement

title: SmartMaint - Predictive Maintenance System
description:
## Problem

Unplanned equipment failures cause expensive production downtime and emergency repairs. Traditional reactive maintenance means teams only act after something breaks, leading to lost revenue and rushed fixes.

## Solution

I built SmartMaint, an AI-driven predictive maintenance system that uses Random Forest models trained on IoT sensor data to forecast equipment failures before they happen. An interactive Streamlit dashboard lets maintenance teams monitor machine health in real time and schedule interventions proactively.

## Architecture

- **ML Engine:** Random Forest classifier built with scikit-learn, trained on multi-sensor IoT time-series data.
- **Frontend:** Streamlit web app providing real-time anomaly alerts, confidence scores, and historical trend visualizations.
- **Data Pipeline:** Pandas and NumPy for sensor data preprocessing and feature engineering.

## Challenges

IoT sensor data was noisy and contained gaps from hardware faults — I had to design robust imputation and outlier-handling strategies without masking genuine anomaly signals. Balancing model accuracy against inference speed was critical for real-time monitoring, and tuning the Random Forest to avoid false alarms while maintaining high recall required extensive cross-validation.

## Results

- **92% accuracy** in predicting equipment failures
- Real-time anomaly detection enabling proactive maintenance scheduling
- Demonstrated how lightweight ML models can deliver production-grade reliability without cloud dependency

title: Statistical Analysis of Road Accidents in Morocco
description:
## Problem

Road accidents are a leading cause of death in Morocco, yet traffic safety decisions were often made without data-driven evidence. There was no accessible tool to visualize where and when accidents concentrate, making it hard to target interventions effectively.

## Solution

I conducted a comprehensive statistical analysis of over 50,000 accident records, building interactive geospatial dashboards that map high-risk zones and reveal temporal traffic patterns. A Tkinter desktop application makes the analysis accessible to non-technical stakeholders.

## Architecture

- **Data Layer:** Pandas for data cleaning and aggregation, GeoPandas for spatial operations on regional boundaries.
- **Visualization:** Matplotlib for static charts and interactive map overlays identifying accident hotspots.
- **Interface:** Custom Tkinter GUI allowing users to filter by region, time period, and severity without writing code.

## Challenges

The raw accident dataset had inconsistent location encoding and missing severity labels — cleaning and geocoding required building a custom normalization pipeline. Rendering geospatial data interactively in a lightweight desktop app without a web server pushed Tkinter to its limits, requiring creative use of Matplotlib's embedding capabilities.

## Results

- Actionable heatmaps identifying high-risk roads and peak accident hours
- Interactive tool enabling policy makers and analysts to explore data without programming skills
- Evidence-based foundation for targeted road safety interventions

title: Tech Horizon - Full-Stack Web Application
description:
## Problem

Online publications need to manage content, users, and analytics in one place — but most platforms force a choice between simplicity and role-based access control. Content teams waste time on manual publishing workflows and lack visibility into article performance.

## Solution

I developed Tech Horizon, a full-stack online magazine with a four-tier role system (visitor, subscriber, manager, admin). Each role has tailored permissions for reading, publishing, managing, and analyzing content — all within a single unified dashboard.

## Architecture

- **Backend:** Laravel (PHP) with MySQL for structured content and user management.
- **Frontend:** Vanilla JavaScript with AJAX for smooth, page-free interactions and HTML5/CSS3 for responsive layout.
- **Features:** Real-time statistics dashboard, secure authentication with role guards, and article lifecycle management.

## Challenges

Designing the four-tier permission model required careful database schema planning to avoid role conflicts and ensure query efficiency. Building real-time analytics without a dedicated frontend framework meant optimizing AJAX polling and server-side aggregation queries. Maintaining clean separation between public-facing pages and the admin dashboard within a monolithic Laravel app demanded disciplined MVC architecture.

## Results

- Complete content management workflow from draft to publication
- Role-based access control serving four distinct user types
- Real-time statistics giving editors visibility into article performance
- Fully documented system (PDF report) suitable for academic evaluation

title: CART - Prédiction de Consommation Énergétique
description:
## Problem

Smart buildings generate vast amounts of sensor data, but without predictive models, energy waste goes unnoticed until utility bills arrive. Building managers need tools to forecast consumption and identify the drivers of energy use.

## Solution

I implemented a full CART (Classification And Regression Tree) pipeline to predict appliance energy consumption from environmental sensor readings. The project covers every stage: exploratory data analysis, preprocessing, feature engineering, model training, hyperparameter tuning, and business-ready interpretation of results.

## Architecture

- **ML Pipeline:** CART decision tree regressor built with scikit-learn, trained on 17,597 cleaned observations from the UCI Appliance Energy dataset.
- **Feature Engineering:** Temporal features (hour-of-day dominated importance), rolling averages, and interaction terms derived from temperature and humidity sensors.
- **Evaluation:** Comprehensive metrics suite including R², MAE, RMSE, residual analysis, and feature importance ranking.

## Challenges

The target variable (appliance energy) had a highly skewed distribution, requiring careful transformation and outlier treatment. Feature importance analysis revealed that hour-of-day dwarfed all physical sensor readings, forcing a rethink of which features actually drive predictions. Balancing model interpretability (a key strength of decision trees) against predictive performance meant accepting a moderate R² in exchange for fully explainable results.

## Results

- **R² = 0.34, MAE = 15.61 Wh, RMSE = 21.62 Wh** on test data
- Decision tree visualization providing full transparency into prediction logic
- Actionable insight: temporal patterns dominate energy use, guiding smarter scheduling strategies
- Fully documented with a 12-page PDF report including methodology and business recommendations

title: Markus Pub Website
description:
## Problem

Markus Pub needed a professional online presence to establish credibility for their project management platform. A generic template wouldn't capture their brand identity, and they required a site that felt polished, modern, and uniquely theirs.

## Solution

I designed and developed a custom brand website that tells Markus Pub's story through immersive sections: team profiles, mission statements, and creative showcases. Every interaction is enhanced with scroll-triggered animations and smooth transitions that make the brand feel alive.

## Architecture

- **Framework:** Next.js with TypeScript for type-safe, SEO-optimized pages.
- **Styling:** TailwindCSS for utility-first responsive design.
- **Animations:** AOS (Animate On Scroll) library for staggered entrance animations and scroll-driven storytelling.
- **Deployment:** Vercel for zero-config continuous deployment and edge caching.

## Challenges

Coordinating multiple animation libraries without causing layout shifts or performance degradation on mobile required careful lazy-loading and animation throttling. Translating the client's abstract brand vision into concrete UI components involved multiple design iterations and close feedback loops.

## Results

- Live production site deployed on Vercel with 100% Lighthouse performance score
- Responsive design delivering consistent brand experience across devices
- Client-ready website that elevated Markus Pub's professional image

title: WhatsApp Chat Analyzer
description:
## Problem

WhatsApp groups accumulate thousands of messages, but the platform offers zero analytics. Users curious about who sends the most messages, which emojis dominate, or when activity peaks have no built-in way to explore their chat data.

## Solution

I built an interactive Streamlit app that transforms raw WhatsApp export files into rich visual analytics. Users upload their chat export and instantly explore top senders, word frequency, emoji usage distributions, and message activity timelines.

## Architecture

- **Parser:** Custom regex-based parser that handles WhatsApp's varying export formats across languages and device types.
- **Analysis:** Pandas for aggregation and statistical summaries.
- **Visualization:** Altair for interactive charts with hover details and zoom.
- **Deployment:** Streamlit Cloud for one-click public access.

## Challenges

WhatsApp export formats differ between iOS and Android, and between languages (date formats, media markers, system messages) — building a parser robust enough to handle all variants without crashing was the core engineering challenge. Multilingual text data (Arabic, French, English mixed in Moroccan chats) complicated word frequency analysis and required Unicode-aware tokenization.

## Results

- Live app deployed on Streamlit Cloud, accessible to anyone with a chat export
- Handles group chats with thousands of messages and dozens of participants
- Surfaces behavioral patterns invisible in the native WhatsApp interface

title: Titanic Survivor Prediction
description:
## Problem

Understanding how different ML algorithms work under the hood is difficult when relying solely on library calls. The best way to learn is to build algorithms from scratch and compare them against optimized implementations — but few projects do both systematically.

## Solution

I implemented K-Nearest Neighbors and Random Forest classifiers twice: once from scratch using only NumPy (to understand the math), and once using scikit-learn (to benchmark against production-grade code). The Titanic dataset provides an intuitive, real-world classification problem to compare both approaches.

## Architecture

- **From-scratch implementations:** Pure Python + NumPy for KNN (distance calculations, voting) and Random Forest (bootstrapping, decision tree construction, ensemble aggregation).
- **Library implementations:** scikit-learn for optimized, hyperparameter-tuned versions of both algorithms.
- **Evaluation:** Side-by-side comparison of accuracy, precision, recall, and training time.

## Challenges

Building a Random Forest from scratch required implementing bootstrap sampling, decision tree splitting with Gini impurity, and ensemble voting — each step had to be mathematically correct and computationally reasonable for a dataset of 891 passengers. Debugging custom implementations against scikit-learn outputs revealed subtle differences in default hyperparameters that affected comparisons.

## Results

- Working implementations of KNN and Random Forest from first principles
- Detailed performance comparison between custom and library versions
- 12-page illustrated PDF report documenting methodology and learnings

title: Watchly AI
description:
## Problem

Discovering what to watch across dozens of streaming platforms is fragmented and frustrating. Users juggle multiple apps to search for titles, track what they've watched, and share recommendations with friends — there's no single, elegant hub for movie and TV discovery.

## Solution

I built Watchly, an all-in-one movie and TV discovery platform powered by TMDB's live database. Users search millions of titles, build and manage personal watchlists, and share recommendations — all within a fast, visually rich interface.

## Architecture

- **Framework:** Next.js with TypeScript for server-side rendering and SEO.
- **Styling:** TailwindCSS with Framer Motion for smooth page transitions and micro-interactions.
- **Data:** TMDB REST API for real-time movie/TV metadata, localStorage for watchlist persistence without requiring accounts.
- **Deployment:** Vercel with edge caching to minimize API latency.

## Challenges

Designing a localStorage-based watchlist that survives browser clears and syncs across tabs required careful state management. Rate-limiting from TMDB's free API tier meant implementing request debouncing and smart caching strategies. Balancing SEO requirements (server-rendered movie pages) with client-side interactivity demanded hybrid rendering patterns in Next.js.

## Results

- Production app deployed on Vercel with full SEO for movie/TV pages
- Seamless search across millions of TMDB titles with debounced live results
- Responsive design delivering a native-app feel on mobile and desktop

title: Dragon Tasks - Chrome Sidebar Extension
description:
## Problem

Productivity tools are fragmented: task managers live in one tab, Pomodoro timers in another, and analytics are nowhere to be found. Chrome users lose focus constantly switching between tools — the friction of context-switching undermines the productivity the tools are supposed to enable.

## Solution

I built Dragon Tasks, a Chrome sidebar extension that unifies task management, a Pomodoro timer, and a productivity analytics dashboard into one always-visible panel. Drag-and-drop reordering, smart filtering, due dates, and auto-save make it a complete productivity companion that lives right next to your work.

## Architecture

- **Platform:** Chrome Extension Manifest V3 with sidebar panel integration.
- **Frontend:** Vanilla JavaScript, CSS, and HTML — no framework, keeping the extension lightweight and fast to load.
- **Storage:** Chrome Storage API for persistent, synced data across browser sessions.
- **Visualization:** Canvas-based charts for the analytics dashboard.

## Challenges

Building within Chrome's sidebar constraints limited available screen real estate — every UI element had to earn its place through iterative usability testing. Implementing reliable drag-and-drop reordering with persistence required careful index management and conflict resolution. The Pomodoro timer had to continue running accurately even when the sidebar was closed, requiring background service worker coordination.

## Results

- All-in-one sidebar combining tasks, focus timer, and productivity stats
- Dark/light mode with system preference detection
- Instant auto-save via Chrome Storage API — zero data loss
- Fully functional Chrome extension ready for the Chrome Web Store

title: Alghaarib Portfolio - Creative Portfolio with Local CMS
description:
## Problem

Creative professionals — designers, videographers, photographers — need a portfolio that showcases their work beautifully. But most portfolio platforms either lock content management behind paid external CMS services or require technical skills to update. Creatives waste time fighting tools instead of showcasing their work.

## Solution

I built Alghaarib, a portfolio platform with a built-in local CMS that lets creatives manage their entire portfolio without touching code. Add projects, upload images, embed YouTube videos with automatic thumbnail extraction, and see changes instantly — all through an intuitive admin dashboard.

## Architecture

- **Frontend:** React with Vite for instant HMR and fast builds.
- **Styling:** TailwindCSS for responsive, polished design across all devices.
- **CMS:** Custom local content management system storing data client-side, eliminating backend hosting costs.
- **Media:** YouTube Data API integration for automatic thumbnail fetching and video embedding.

## Challenges

Building a functional CMS without a backend server required designing a robust local data model and file management strategy. YouTube thumbnail extraction demanded parsing video IDs from various URL formats (shorts, embeds, watch links). Ensuring real-time preview updates when adding or editing projects without page refreshes required careful React state architecture.

## Results

- Fully functional portfolio with local CMS — no backend, no hosting costs
- YouTube video integration with automatic thumbnail extraction
- Responsive design optimized for desktop, tablet, and mobile
- Production deployment on Vercel serving a live creative portfolio

title: Ant Lion Optimizer for Permutation Flow Shop Scheduling
description:
## Overview

A research-driven implementation of the **Ant Lion Optimizer (ALO)** for the **Permutation Flow Shop Scheduling Problem (PFSP)**, one of the most challenging NP-hard optimization problems in manufacturing and production planning.

The project adapts the original continuous ALO algorithm to permutation-based scheduling using the **Random Keys** representation, allowing it to optimize job sequences while preserving the algorithm's original exploration and exploitation mechanisms. Performance is evaluated on **Taillard benchmark instances** and compared against **Genetic Algorithms** and **Random Search** through rigorous statistical benchmarking.

### Highlights

* 🐜 Faithful implementation of the original **Ant Lion Optimizer (Mirjalili, 2015)**.
* 🔄 Random Keys encoding for continuous-to-permutation optimization.
* ⚙️ Efficient makespan computation for Flow Shop Scheduling.
* 📊 Benchmarking against Genetic Algorithm and Random Search.
* 📈 Statistical analysis with convergence curves, box plots, Gantt charts, and scalability experiments.
* 🏭 Evaluation on industry-standard **Taillard** scheduling benchmarks.

This project demonstrates how a continuous metaheuristic can be successfully adapted to solve complex combinatorial scheduling problems through a minimal yet elegant modification, achieving competitive results while preserving the integrity of the original algorithm.

title: My Personal Portfolio
description:
## Overview

A high-performance portfolio designed to showcase AI, Data Science, and full-stack projects through technical case studies instead of static listings. It includes a custom CMS for managing projects and blog posts while maintaining excellent performance, accessibility, and SEO.

## Problem

Traditional portfolios quickly become outdated, focus on technologies rather than impact, and provide little insight into the developer's engineering process. Updating content often requires modifying the source code.

## Solution

Built a modern portfolio with:

- Interactive project case studies
- Password-protected admin dashboard
- Live CRUD content management
- Technical blog
- Performance-first architecture
- Responsive and accessible design

## Architecture

**Frontend:** React, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS, Framer Motion, Three.js

**Backend:** Bun API with JSON-based storage for lightweight content management and easy deployment.

## Challenges

- Optimized large images, reducing payload by over **90%**.
- Eliminated layout shifts through improved font loading.
- Built a lightweight CMS without relying on a database.
- Improved accessibility by fixing Lighthouse WCAG issues.

## Results

- 🚀 Performance: **99**
- ♿ Accessibility: **91**
- ✅ Best Practices: **100**
- 🔍 SEO: **100**

Additional improvements include faster page loads, near-zero layout shift, responsive design, and simplified content management.

## What I Learned

This project strengthened my skills in frontend performance optimization, accessibility, animation, headless CMS architecture, and full-stack development with Bun. Most importantly, it taught me that a portfolio should communicate engineering decisions and project impact, not just showcase finished work.

title: DataNova: Ai data science platform
description:
## Overview

DataNova is an end-to-end machine learning platform that streamlines the complete data science workflow, from dataset exploration to model training, prediction, and automated reporting. Instead of operating as a black-box AutoML system, it provides explainable algorithm recommendations and a guided workflow that helps users understand **why** a model is suggested.

Designed for students, researchers, analysts, and data scientists, the platform combines dataset management, preprocessing, experimentation, visualization, prediction, and report generation into a single unified environment.

---

## The Problem

Machine learning platforms often require significant expertise and expose users to fragmented workflows. Selecting the right algorithm, preparing data, tracking experiments, and documenting results are typically manual processes. Existing AutoML solutions prioritize prediction accuracy but rarely explain the reasoning behind their recommendations.

---

## The Solution

DataNova delivers a guided machine learning workflow that makes model development more accessible and reproducible. The platform analyzes datasets, recommends suitable algorithms with transparent reasoning, manages preprocessing through configurable pipelines, tracks every experiment, and automatically generates structured reports for academic and professional use.

---

## Core Features

- 📂 Dataset management and exploration
- 🧪 Visual preprocessing pipeline builder
- 🤖 Explainable algorithm recommendation engine
- 📊 Statistical data visualization
- ⚙️ Model training and experiment management
- 🔮 Model prediction interface
- 📝 Automated report generation
- 📜 Complete activity logging and experiment history

---

## Architecture

The application follows a modular full-stack architecture with clear separation between presentation, application, and persistence layers. Machine learning components are abstracted into reusable services, allowing new algorithms and workflows to be integrated without affecting the overall system design.

---

## Challenges

Developing a unified interface capable of supporting multiple machine learning algorithms while maintaining transparency was one of the project's primary challenges. Additional complexity came from handling long-running training tasks, reusable preprocessing pipelines, experiment tracking, and automated documentation without compromising the user experience.

---

## Results

DataNova provides a complete machine learning workspace where users can manage datasets, preprocess data, train models, compare algorithms, generate predictions, and produce structured reports from a single application. By emphasizing explainability over automation, the platform improves both workflow consistency and user understanding of machine learning models.

---

## What I Learned

Building DataNova strengthened my understanding of:

- Designing scalable full-stack architectures
- Developing modular and maintainable machine learning systems
- Building explainable AI workflows
- Managing asynchronous tasks and long-running processes
- Structuring reusable preprocessing pipelines
- Experiment tracking and reproducibility
- Creating user-centered developer tools instead of focusing solely on model performance
